## ARTICLE 1. RECITALS AND DEFINITIONS

### Section 1.1 Recitals.

**1.1.1** The City is the owner of certain real property commonly known as 1400–1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, consisting of three (3) parcels of record identified by Parcel Identification Numbers 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) (collectively, the "Property" or "Site"). The Property constitutes the former site of the Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased industrial operations in 2009, and is situated immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station on the Metra Milwaukee District/North Line.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C4 (parcel acquisition history). The Resolution, the TIF Memo, and the RFP Response state that the City acquired Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-016 through tax forfeiture proceedings concluded in 2016, and acquired Parcel 12-24-300-017 by direct purchase in 2018. The Phase II ESA (Section 1.3) states that Parcel 12-24-300-015 was acquired by tax forfeiture in 2016, while Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017 were acquired by direct purchase in 2018. This discrepancy is material because tax-forfeiture parcels may carry different title characteristics (e.g., unresolved tax claims, different warranty posture) than direct-purchase parcels, which affects the City's ability to deliver insurable title and the scope of the City's special warranty deed covenants. ## ARTICLE 2. EFFECTIVENESS; CONDITIONS PRECEDENT TO EXECUTION

### Section 2.1 Effectiveness.

This Agreement shall become effective upon the date of its full execution by both parties and approval by the Lakemont City Council at a duly noticed public meeting (the "Effective Date").

### Section 2.2 Conditions Precedent to Execution.

The execution and delivery of this Agreement by Developer is subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by Developer) of the following conditions precedent:

(a) **Rezoning.** The rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development–Transit Oriented District) shall have been approved by the City Council by ordinance and shall be final and effective, with the applicable appeal period expired without the filing of any appeal, or any appeal finally resolved in a manner that confirms the rezoning.

(b) **Parcel Consolidation.** The consolidation of Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 into a single parcel of record shall have been completed or authorized by the applicable county authority, subject to the title review recommended in the Phase II ESA.

(c) **IEPA Remedial Action Plan.** IEPA shall have approved a RAP for Parcel 12-24-300-016 under the VSRP, addressing the BTEX and TCE contamination identified in the Phase II ESA.

(d) **Equity Demonstration.** Developer shall have demonstrated committed equity of not less than $85,000,000, in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City, including executed commitment letters from equity partners, evidence of available and uncommitted capital, bank statements, or other documentation satisfactory to the City in its reasonable discretion, verified by an independent certification from Developer's auditing firm, Clearmont Accounting Group LLP.

(e) **City Council Approval.** The Lakemont City Council shall have approved this Agreement.

(f) **Construction Lender Confirmation.** The Construction Lender shall have confirmed in writing that this Agreement is in form and substance acceptable to it as a condition of the Construction Loan commitment.

## ARTICLE 3. THE PROJECT

### Section 3.1 Project Description.

The Project consists of a mixed-use, transit-oriented development organized in four phases, with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet, comprising the program elements set forth in this Article 3 and in Exhibit [__] (Project Description and Phasing Plan). The Project is designed as a cohesive, master-planned transit-oriented development serving as a catalyst for revitalization of the Lakemont Central station area.

### Section 3.2 Phase I — Residential Tower A and Retail Podium.

Phase I comprises: (a) three hundred twenty (320) residential dwelling units, consisting of two hundred fifty-six (256) market-rate units and sixty-four (64) Affordable Units; (b) forty-five thousand (45,000) square feet of ground-floor retail space; and (c) a four hundred eighty (480)-space structured parking facility. Phase I comprises approximately four hundred twenty thousand (420,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $105,000,000. Phase I groundbreaking shall occur no later than March 1, 2026, with Substantial Completion by September 30, 2028 (approximately thirty (30) months of construction).

### Section 3.3 Phase II — Office Building and Retail.

Phase II comprises: (a) two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; (b) eighteen thousand (18,000) square feet of retail space; and (c) a three hundred fifty (350)-space structured parking facility. Phase II comprises approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $76,000,000. Phase II shall commence within twelve (12) months of Phase I Substantial Completion, with Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase II commencement.

### Section 3.4 Phase III — Residential Tower B and Hotel.

Phase III comprises: (a) two hundred ten (210) residential dwelling units, consisting of one hundred sixty-eight (168) market-rate units and forty-two (42) Affordable Units; and (b) a one hundred fifty (150)-room select-service hotel. Phase III comprises approximately four hundred forty thousand (440,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $88,000,000. Phase III shall commence within twelve (12) months of Phase II Substantial Completion, with Substantial Completion within thirty (30) months of Phase III commencement.

[Drafting Note — Open item O1 (hotel brand). The Term Sheet (Section 3.3) states the hotel brand affiliation is "to be determined" and that Developer is in discussions with two national hospitality flags. The Agreement should preserve Developer's flexibility to select the hotel brand based on prevailing market conditions at the time of Phase III execution, subject only to compliance with the PD-TOD zoning and the LEED Gold commitment. No City approval of the specific brand should be required.]

### Section 3.5 Phase IV — Public Plaza, Community Center, and Parking Structure.

Phase IV comprises: (a) a 2.5-acre public plaza incorporating landscaping and public art installations; (b) a twenty-eight thousand (28,000) square foot community center facility to be conveyed to the City upon completion as provided in Section 3.7; and (c) a six hundred (600)-space public parking structure. Phase IV comprises approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of $71,000,000. Phase IV shall commence concurrently with Phase III, with Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase IV commencement.

### Section 3.6 Project Summary Totals.

In the aggregate, the Project shall include: five hundred thirty (530) residential dwelling units, of which one hundred six (106) shall be Affordable Units; sixty-three thousand (63,000) square feet of total retail space; two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; one hundred fifty (150) hotel rooms; one thousand four hundred thirty (1,430) structured parking spaces; a 2.5-acre public plaza; and a 28,000 square foot community center. The total estimated hard construction cost for all four phases is approximately $340,000,000, and the total estimated Project cost, inclusive of soft costs, is approximately $412,000,000.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C8 (affordable unit count). The Resolution and the detailed breakdown in Term Sheet Section 12 specify 106 Affordable Units (64 in Phase I + 42 in Phase III), representing 20% of 530 total residential units. The Term Sheet Section 3.5 summary table, however, states "at least 100 affordable units." The Agreement states 106 units, which exceeds both the City's 20% minimum (Resolution Condition #8(g)) and the "at least 100" floor in the Term Sheet summary. Confirm the City accepts 106 as the binding number and that the "at least 100" language in the Term Sheet summary is superseded.]

### Section 3.7 Community Center Conveyance.

The community center shall be designed in consultation with the City's Parks and Recreation Department. Upon Substantial Completion of Phase IV, Developer shall convey the community center to the City in fee simple by special warranty deed, at no additional cost to the City beyond the costs included in the Project Budget. The community center shall be delivered in accordance with the design specifications agreed by the parties in Exhibit [__].

The Term Sheet (Section 3.4) states the community center is "to be designed in consultation with the City's Parks and Recreation Department and conveyed to the City upon completion at no additional cost." ### Section 3.8 Public Parking Structure.

The 600-space public parking structure in Phase IV shall serve Metra commuters, retail and restaurant patrons, community center and plaza visitors, and Project residents and workers, and shall be publicly accessible. The operating and access arrangements for the public parking structure shall be set forth in an access/operating agreement in Exhibit [__], which shall confirm the public character of the facility for TIF eligibility purposes.

[Drafting Note — Open item O6 (public parking operating arrangements). The TIF Memo (Section III.B) notes that the Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center are eligible as construction of public improvements and facilities, and that the Phase I (480-space) and Phase II (350-space) garages are "potentially eligible to the extent they are publicly accessible or contractually committed to public use," requiring analysis of access agreements and deed restrictions. Developer shall submit design plans for each Phase to the City's Community Development Department for review and approval. The City's review shall be limited to confirming consistency with the PD-TOD zoning standards, the Project Description, and applicable building codes. The City's approval rights shall not extend to aesthetic matters within Developer's discretion or to matters that would increase Project cost or delay the schedule, in each case without the City bearing the resulting cost or extending equivalent schedule relief.

## ARTICLE 4. LAND CONVEYANCE

### Section 4.1 Conveyance; Purchase Price.

Following the Effective Date and satisfaction of the conditions precedent to closing set forth in Section 4.3, the City shall convey the Property (as consolidated into a single parcel, or in phased conveyances as provided in Section 4.2) to Developer by special warranty deed, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and exceptions except Permitted Exceptions. The purchase price is $9,200,000, representing 50% of the appraised as-is market value of $18,400,000 (Crossland Appraisal Services, dated January 15, 2025), resulting in a land write-down incentive of $9,200,000. The purchase price shall be due and payable at closing, and Developer may fund it from equity, Construction Loan proceeds, or a combination thereof.

### Section 4.2 Phased Conveyance.

Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, Developer may elect, by notice to the City, to receive conveyance of the Property in phases: (a) Parcel 12-24-300-015 (which requires no remediation) and Parcel 12-24-300-017 (subject to completion of the engineered barrier) may be conveyed first; and (b) Parcel 12-24-300-016 may be conveyed after issuance of the NFR Letter for that parcel. A phased conveyance shall be coordinated with the PD-TOD rezoning, parcel consolidation, and the Construction Lender's requirements regarding the scope and legal description of the mortgaged premises.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C15/C36 (phased conveyance). The Phase II ESA (Sections 4.1.3, 5.2, 8.2) recommends a phased conveyance approach to allow Phase I construction to begin on clean Parcels 015 and 017 while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds, noting that this requires (i) PD-TOD rezoning and consolidation to accommodate phased delivery or separate/interim land use approvals, (ii) resolution of per-parcel title issues, and (iii) coordination with the Construction Lender's mortgage scope. Resolution Condition #8(b) requires IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 "prior to any conveyance of the Property or any portion thereof," which on its face permits conveyance of clean parcels once the RAP is approved (it does not require the NFR Letter before conveyance of any parcel). The City should confirm that phased conveyance is permitted and that the rezoning/consolidation can accommodate it. This is a key schedule-risk mitigation for Developer.]

### Section 4.3 Conditions Precedent to Closing.

The obligation of each party to close the conveyance is subject to satisfaction (or waiver by the waiving party) of the following conditions precedent:

(a) Execution and delivery of this Agreement and City Council approval;
(b) Completion of rezoning of the Property from M-2 to PD-TOD;
(c) Completion or authorization of parcel consolidation (or, in the case of a phased conveyance, the applicable parcel(s));
(d) IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 12-24-300-016;
(e) Developer's demonstration of committed equity of at least $85,000,000;
(f) Developer's posting of the $15,000,000 Phase I performance security under Article 8;
(g) Delivery by the City of an owner's title insurance commitment insuring marketable and insurable fee simple title in Developer, subject only to Permitted Exceptions, together with a current ALTA/NSPS land title survey; and
(h) Delivery by the City of the Construction Lender's confirmation that this Agreement is in form acceptable to it.

### Section 4.4 Title; Insurability.

The City shall deliver marketable and insurable fee simple absolute title to the Property, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, restrictions, reversionary interests, rights of re-entry, possibilities of reverter, defeasible conditions, and other title defects or exceptions, except Permitted Exceptions. The City shall provide a standard owner's title insurance policy in the amount of the purchase price. The Property conveyed to Developer shall not be subject to any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate in favor of the City or any other party.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C1 (reversionary interest / defeasible fee). This Section 4.4, together with Article 14, implements the Construction Lender's absolute requirement (Loan Term Sheet Sections 4.2, 7.1(1), 12(a)) that the Property not be subject to any reversionary or defeasible interest. The Construction Lender states that any such interest would render the title uninsurable, subordinate the mortgage to the City's right to reclaim the property, prevent foreclosure, and render the Construction Loan unsecurable — and that incorporation of Resolution Condition #8(h)'s reversionary interest "as stated in the Resolution, would make the Loan impossible to close." Resolution Condition #8(h), however, requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest with automatic reversion if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. This is the central cross-document conflict. Article 14 substitutes an alternative completion-assurance mechanism (a contractual repurchase option plus enhanced performance security) that is intended to satisfy the City's policy objective of ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest. The City must agree to deviate from the literal terms of Resolution Condition #8(h); this requires either (i) a further City Council action amending or superseding Condition #8(h), or (ii) a determination by Corporation Counsel that the alternative mechanism satisfies the condition. This is the single most important open item and must be resolved before execution.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C2 (36-month reversion clock vs. 30-month construction). Even setting aside the title-insurability problem, Resolution Condition #8(h) measures the 36-month reversion period from the date of conveyance, while Phase I Substantial Completion is targeted for September 30, 2028 with groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026. If conveyance occurs in mid-2025 (the Term Sheet's target closing date), the 36-month period could expire before the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline, meaning the reversion could be triggered through no fault of Developer. ## ARTICLE 6. ENVIRONMENTAL MATTERS

### Section 6.1 Environmental Conditions.

The environmental conditions of the Property are as documented in the Phase II ESA prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, dated November 8, 2024, and are summarized as follows:

(a) **Parcel 12-24-300-015.** No environmental contamination requiring further action was identified; no further investigation or remediation is required.

(b) **Parcel 12-24-300-016.** BTEX contamination in soil and groundwater over approximately 1.8 acres, and localized TCE contamination, attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations (former UST farm and metal finishing/degreasing operations). Remediation under the IEPA VSRP is required, at an estimated cost of $2,800,000 and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in issuance of an NFR Letter.

(c) **Parcel 12-24-300-017.** Low-level metals contamination (primarily arsenic) in shallow fill material, suitable for residential use with installation of an engineered barrier (clean fill cap), at an estimated cost of $340,000.

The total estimated environmental remediation cost is $3,140,000.

### Section 6.2 City Indemnification of Pre-Existing Environmental Conditions.

The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property (Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017), including without limitation third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws, and any contamination attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations that ceased in 2009. The contamination identified in the Phase II ESA is pre-existing and attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations; the City acquired the affected parcels with knowledge of the environmental conditions. This indemnity shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement and the conveyance of the Property.

### Section 6.3 Remediation Responsibility and Cost Allocation.

(a) **Performance.** Developer shall perform, or contract for, the remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016 under the VSRP and the engineered barrier installation on Parcel 12-24-300-017, using Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC or another qualified environmental consultant. Developer shall be the named applicant on the VSRP enrollment and NFR Letter application for Parcel 12-24-300-016, except as the parties may otherwise agree.

(b) **Base Cost.** The base estimated environmental remediation cost is $3,140,000 ($2,800,000 for Parcel 016 and $340,000 for Parcel 017). Environmental remediation costs are eligible TIF costs and shall be reimbursed to Developer on a first-priority basis under Section 5.2(d).

(c) **Cost Overruns.** The Phase II ESA (Section 5.3) warns that actual remediation costs may exceed the base estimate by 25% to 35% (with total Parcel 016 cost exposure of approximately $3,500,000 to $3,800,000, and combined total exposure of approximately $3,800,000 to $4,200,000 in a significant-scope-expansion scenario), due to potential discovery of additional contamination, extended groundwater treatment, or supplemental regulatory requirements. Any environmental remediation cost exceeding the base estimate of $3,140,000 shall be borne by the City, or, at Developer's election, shall be reimbursed to Developer as an eligible TIF cost on a first-priority basis. The City shall not be entitled to offset such overruns against any other incentive or against the purchase price.

(d) **Vapor Intrusion.** If the vapor intrusion screening evaluation recommended in the Phase II ESA (Sections 7.1, 8.1) indicates that vapor mitigation measures (such as sub-slab depressurization systems, vapor barriers, or passive venting systems) are warranted for structures on or adjacent to the contaminated area of Parcel 12-24-300-016, the cost of such vapor mitigation measures shall be borne by the City or reimbursed to Developer as an eligible TIF cost, as a pre-existing environmental condition.

(e) **Off-Site Investigation.** If IEPA requires off-site groundwater monitoring (including on or adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station property, as recommended in the Phase II ESA Section 8.3), the City shall assist Developer in obtaining any required access from Metra or other third parties, and the cost of such off-site investigation shall be borne by the City or reimbursed as an eligible TIF cost.

(f) **Pre-Remediation Plume Delineation.** The Phase II ESA (Section 8.3) recommends the advancement of six to eight (6–8) additional soil borings on the periphery of the Parcel 12-24-300-016 BTEX plume to refine the lateral and vertical boundaries of the excavation zone prior to finalization of the RAP, in order to reduce the risk of encountering unexpected contamination during excavation and to improve the reliability of the remediation cost estimate. The cost of such pre-remediation plume delineation shall be borne by the City or reimbursed to Developer as an eligible TIF cost, as a pre-existing environmental condition investigation cost.

Developer's indemnification of the City for prevailing wage violations is set forth in Section 17.5. As a remedy for a material, uncured prevailing wage violation on TIF-reimbursed components, the City may withhold TIF reimbursement payments otherwise due to Developer under Article 5 until the violation is cured; provided, that (i) the City shall not withhold TIF payments for violations on components that are not subject to the Prevailing Wage Act by statute or by the parties' contractual agreement in Exhibit [__], and (ii) the City shall not withhold more than the documented amount of the violation plus penalties, and shall release withheld amounts promptly upon cure.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C42 (TIF withholding as prevailing wage remedy). The TIF Memo (Section V.C) recommends that the development agreement include, among prevailing wage compliance provisions, "specified remedies for non-compliance, including withholding of TIF reimbursement payments," together with a Developer covenant, monthly certified payroll submission, City audit rights, and a Developer indemnification of the City. Section 9.3(d) and Section 17.5 implement these recommendations. Developer's position, reflected in Section 9.3(d), is to (i) limit the TIF-withholding remedy to TIF-reimbursed components where prevailing wage applies (by statute or by the parties' express contractual agreement), (ii) cap the withholding at the documented violation amount plus penalties, and (iii) require prompt release upon cure — so that the City cannot withhold TIF reimbursement for the entire Project based on a violation on a single component, and cannot withhold for components outside the prevailing wage scope. The City may seek broader withholding rights; reconcile. The Resolution (Section 8) requires the Development Agreement to include prevailing wage "record-keeping, reporting, and certification requirements," which Sections 9.3(b) and (d) address.]

[Drafting Note — Conflict C13 (prevailing wage scope). The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act." The TIF Memo (Section V) concludes that, given the substantial public financial assistance (land write-down, TIF, abatement, fee waivers), the Prevailing Wage Act "likely applies to all construction activity on the project site, not merely the TIF-reimbursed components," and recommends the development agreement specify the scope with precision. The TIF Memo notes that the Term Sheet's "all construction" language "converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation" and that the Developer "should understand that this provision may increase construction costs for components... that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate." Developer's position, reflected in Section 9.3, is to (i) comply with the Prevailing Wage Act where it applies by statute, (ii) flag the debatability of full-project coverage, and (iii) seek offsetting incentive adjustments if the parties contractually extend coverage to purely private components. The City's strongest position (per the TIF Memo) is broad application. This is a significant cost open item; the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 3.5) prohibits Developer from agreeing to terms materially increasing total Project costs beyond $412,000,000 without Investor consent, so any contractual extension of prevailing wage that materially increases cost may require Lakeshore consent.]

### Section 9.4 Insurance.

Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each Phase, the following minimum insurance coverages: (a) Commercial General Liability (CGL) — $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate; (b) Umbrella/Excess Liability — $25,000,000; (c) Builder's Risk — full replacement value of each Phase under construction; (d) Workers' Compensation — statutory limits; (e) Automobile Liability — $5,000,000 combined single limit; and (f) Professional Liability (Architect/Engineer) — $5,000,000. The City and the Construction Lender shall be named as additional insureds/loss payees, as applicable. All carriers shall be rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best. All policies shall provide not less than 30 days' prior written notice of cancellation or material modification.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C9 (CGL aggregate). The Term Sheet (Section 11.3) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $20,000,000 in the aggregate. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 5) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate. Section 9.4 adopts the higher aggregate ($25,000,000) required by the Construction Lender, which also satisfies the Term Sheet. The Term Sheet's lower aggregate is superseded by the Construction Lender's requirement. Confirm the City accepts the $25,000,000 aggregate.]

### Section 9.5 Construction Monitoring.

Developer shall submit monthly draw requests supported by AIA G702/G703 documentation to the Construction Lender and shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City's Community Development Department, including percentage of completion, updated schedule, and identification of any delays, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring.

---

## ARTICLE 10. COMMUNITY BENEFITS

### Section 10.1 Local Hiring.

Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to achieve a goal of 35% Lakemont residents for construction workforce employment across all Phases of the Project, measured on a cumulative project-wide basis. Developer shall submit quarterly reports to the City's Community Development Department documenting local hiring percentages within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring. Failure to achieve the 35% local hiring goal in any given quarter or on a cumulative basis shall not, in and of itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided Developer demonstrates good-faith efforts to achieve the goal, including posting job openings with the City's workforce development partners, attending local job fairs, and providing reasonable notice of hiring opportunities to the Community Development Department.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C19 (local hiring — goal vs. requirement). The Term Sheet (Section 9) frames the 35% local hiring commitment as a goal measured on a cumulative project-wide basis, with good-faith compliance and an express statement that failure to achieve the goal "shall not, in and of itself, constitute a default." Resolution Condition #8(c)(i), however, refers to "a local hiring goal requiring that not less than thirty-five percent (35%)." Developer's position, reflected in Section 10.1, is that the 35% commitment is a good-faith goal, not a hard requirement, and that missing the goal is not a default if good-faith efforts are demonstrated. The City may seek to tighten this; reconcile the language.]

### Section 10.2 Living Wage.

Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to require that all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project be compensated at a wage rate of not less than $17.50 per hour, indexed annually for inflation pursuant to Exhibit [__]. This obligation shall apply to employees of commercial tenants and hotel operators, and Developer's obligation shall be limited to including a living-wage covenant in leases and operating agreements with such tenants and operators and using commercially reasonable efforts to enforce it; Developer shall not be personally liable for wage obligations of tenants or operators.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C18 (living wage — new obligation). Resolution Condition #8(c)(ii) requires a Community Benefits Agreement or provisions mandating a living wage of not less than $17.50 per hour for "all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project." This living-wage obligation does not appear in the Term Sheet (which addresses only local hiring) or the RFP Response (which addresses first-source hiring and local hiring but not a living wage). This is a new obligation introduced by the Resolution. Developer's position, reflected in Section 10.2, is to (i) limit the obligation to a lease/operating-agreement covenant enforced through commercially reasonable efforts (rather than direct Developer liability for tenant wages), (ii) index the rate to avoid a fixed nominal amount eroding over time, and (iii) limit scope to retail and hotel permanent employees as stated in the Resolution. The City may seek broader coverage or direct liability; this is an open item. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 3.5) constraint on materially increasing Project costs may be implicated.]

### Section 10.3 First-Source Hiring.

Developer and its commercial tenants shall partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for the recruitment, training, and hiring of individuals to fill permanent employment positions created within the Project, by providing the Workforce Center with notice of available positions prior to broader recruitment. Developer's obligation shall be to maintain a first-source referral relationship with the Workforce Center and to give good-faith consideration to referred candidates; it shall not require Developer to hire any particular candidate or to hire exclusively through the Workforce Center.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C18 (first-source hiring). Resolution Condition #8(c)(iii) requires a first-source hiring program under which Developer and its commercial tenants "shall partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for the recruitment, training, and hiring of individuals to fill permanent employment positions." The RFP Response (Section 6.2) commits to a first-source hiring program. The Term Sheet (Section 9) does not expressly include first-source hiring (it addresses only local hiring). Developer's position, reflected in Section 10.3, is to frame first-source as a good-faith referral/priority-notice program rather than an exclusive or mandatory hiring channel. The City may seek a stronger commitment; reconcile.]

### Section 10.4 Community Benefits Agreement.

The community benefits provisions set forth in this Article 10 shall be deemed to constitute the Community Benefits Agreement or Community Benefits provisions required by Resolution Condition #8(c), and no separate Community Benefits Agreement with third parties shall be required unless the parties otherwise agree in writing.

---

## ARTICLE 11. AFFORDABLE HOUSING

### Section 11.1 Affordable Units.

Developer shall include 106 Affordable Units across the Project, restricted to households earning at or below 60% of AMI, representing 20% of the 530 total residential units. The Affordable Units shall be distributed as follows: 64 Affordable Units in Phase I (out of 320 total residential units) and 42 Affordable Units in Phase III (out of 210 total residential units).

### Section 11.2 Integration and Comparability.

Affordable Units shall be distributed proportionally across unit types (studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom) and shall not be segregated or concentrated on particular floors or in separate wings. Affordable Units shall be comparable in design, finishes, and amenity access to market-rate units within the same building, subject to reasonable variations consistent with industry practice.

### Section 11.3 Affordability Restriction Period.

Affordability restrictions shall remain in effect for a minimum of thirty (30) years from the date of issuance of the certificate of occupancy for the applicable Phase. The affordability restrictions shall be memorialized by a restrictive covenant recorded against the applicable parcel and running with the land, in a form to be set forth in Exhibit [__].

### Section 11.4 Compliance.

Developer shall comply with the City's Affordable Housing Compliance Program and shall provide annual income certification documentation to the City's Community Development Department for all Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C8 (affordable unit count). See the drafting note to Section 3.6. The Agreement states 106 Affordable Units, exceeding the City's 20% minimum (Resolution Condition #8(g)) and the "at least 100" floor in the Term Sheet summary. The affordability covenant form (open item) should be developed in coordination with the Construction Lender and title company to ensure it does not impair title insurability or the Construction Lender's security.]

---

## ARTICLE 12. PHASING, TIMELINES, AND FORCE MAJEURE

### Section 12.1 Phasing Schedule.

The Project shall be constructed in four Phases in accordance with the following schedule:

| Phase | Commencement Deadline | Substantial Completion Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Phase I | Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026 | September 30, 2028 |
| Phase II | Within 12 months of Phase I Substantial Completion | Within 24 months of Phase II commencement |
| Phase III | Within 12 months of Phase II Substantial Completion | Within 30 months of Phase III commencement |
| Phase IV | Concurrent with Phase III commencement | Within 24 months of Phase IV commencement |

The commencement deadline for each subsequent Phase is measured from the Substantial Completion date of the immediately preceding Phase, as may be extended by Force Majeure. Developer shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C44 (Lakeshore 2035 overall-completion consent trigger). The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(c)) requires Investor consent for any modification to phasing timelines that would extend overall Project completion beyond calendar year 2035, and Section 7(c) prohibits Developer, without Investor consent, from modifying the Project phasing schedule in a manner that would defer Phase I Substantial Completion beyond September 30, 2028 (as extended by force majeure) or defer overall Project completion beyond calendar year 2035. Under the contractual maximum schedule in Section 12.1, Phase III Substantial Completion would extend to approximately March 2035 and Phase IV to approximately September 2034 (per the RFP Response Section 10), so the contractual maximums sit at the edge of the Lakeshore 2035 outer boundary. Any Force Majeure extension or delay that pushes overall Project completion beyond 2035 would require Lakeshore consent under the Side Letter, in addition to any City consent. Developer should track the 2035 boundary and obtain Lakeshore consent proactively if delays are anticipated. The Term Sheet (Section 13) and Resolution do not impose a 2035 overall-completion deadline, so the 2035 constraint is an internal Developer (investor) constraint, not a City-imposed one.]

### Section 12.2 Substantial Completion.

"Substantial Completion" of a Phase means the completion of construction of the applicable Phase to the point where the building or improvements can be occupied and used for their intended purpose, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City's Building Department for the applicable building(s). Substantial Completion of a Phase shall not be delayed by punch-list items, cosmetic defects, or minor incomplete work that does not prevent occupancy and use of the building for its intended purpose.

[Drafting Note — Open item O8 (Substantial Completion definition). The Term Sheet (Section 13) states that Substantial Completion "shall be defined in the definitive Agreement" and generally means completion to the point of occupancy, evidenced by a temporary or final certificate of occupancy. Resolution Condition #8(h) references Substantial Completion "as such term shall be defined in the Development Agreement." Section 12.2 defines Substantial Completion in Developer-favorable terms tied to certificate of occupancy issuance and excluding punch-list/cosmetic items. The City may seek a stricter definition; reconcile.]

### Section 12.3 Force Majeure.

(a) **Definition.** "Force Majeure" means acts of God, fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane, tornado, severe weather events, pandemic, epidemic, quarantine, governmental health orders, war, terrorism, civil unrest, insurrection, strikes or labor disputes (other than those caused by Developer's labor practices or failure to comply with applicable labor laws), industry-wide supply chain disruptions beyond Developer's reasonable control, governmental moratoria on construction, actions or inactions of governmental authorities (including IEPA delays in reviewing or approving the RAP or NFR Letter application, and delays in the rezoning or parcel consolidation process), and actions or inactions of the City of Lakemont that prevent or materially delay construction.

(b) **Effect.** Force Majeure events shall extend the applicable deadlines for commencement and Substantial Completion on a day-for-day basis for the duration of the Force Majeure event.

(c) **Aggregate Cap.** The maximum extension of time available under this Section 12.3 shall not exceed eighteen (18) months in the aggregate across the Project.

(d) **Notice.** Developer shall provide written notice to the City within fifteen (15) business days of the occurrence of a Force Majeure event, describing the nature, anticipated duration, and expected impact, with updated notices as circumstances change.

(e) **Mitigation.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate the effects of any Force Majeure event.

(f) **Exclusions.** Force Majeure shall not include financial inability to perform, changes in market conditions, increases in the cost of labor or materials (unless attributable to an industry-wide supply chain disruption), Developer's failure to obtain financing, or any event caused by Developer's negligence or willful misconduct.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C21 (force majeure cap). The Term Sheet (Section 14) provides an 18-month aggregate Force Majeure cap across the Project. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9(9)) provides Force Majeure extensions of "up to 180 days" (six months) for the Phase I Substantial Completion default, plus any Construction Lender-approved extension periods under Section 2.3. There is a gap: the Agreement permits up to 18 months of Force Majeure relief, but the Construction Loan may default after only 180 days (six months) of Force Majeure delay for Phase I. Developer should negotiate with the Construction Lender to align the loan's Force Majeure cap with the Agreement's 18-month cap, or to obtain Construction Lender extension approvals as needed. This is an open item. Section 12.3(a) also expressly includes IEPA review delays and rezoning/consolidation delays as Force Majeure (addressing the Phase II ESA's recommendation, Section 8.2(d), that the transactional documents address whether delay in NFR Letter issuance constitutes Force Majeure or a deadline extension).]

### Section 12.4 Schedule Buffer.

Developer maintains a target accelerated schedule approximately two years ahead of the contractual maximum deadlines set forth in Section 12.1. The contractual maximum deadlines govern for purposes of this Agreement and provide buffer for regulatory, market, and construction contingencies.

---

## ARTICLE 13. DEFAULT AND CURE; TERMINATION

### Section 13.1 Monetary Defaults.

In the event of a monetary default by either party (including Developer's failure to pay the land purchase price or the City's failure to fund committed infrastructure amounts), the non-defaulting party shall provide written notice specifying the nature and amount of the default. The defaulting party shall have sixty (60) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default.

In the event of a non-monetary default by either party (including failure to commence construction within applicable deadlines, failure to comply with affordable housing requirements, or failure to maintain required insurance), the non-defaulting party shall provide written notice specifying the nature of the default in reasonable detail. ### Section 13.3 Termination by City.

The City may terminate this Agreement upon: (a) a material breach by Developer that remains uncured after expiration of the applicable cure period (as extended by the Construction Lender's cure rights under Article 16); (b) Developer's failure to commence Phase I construction within six (6) months following land conveyance; (c) Developer's abandonment of the Project, evidenced by cessation of all construction activity for a period of one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days (other than cessation caused by Force Majeure); and (d) such other events of default as specified in this Agreement. **[1. Introduction and Purpose]{.underline}**

**[1.1 Authorization and Scope]{.underline}**

Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC (\"GEC\") was retained by Granite City Development LLC (\"Developer\") to conduct a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment (\"Phase II ESA\") of three parcels comprising approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) located at 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048 (collectively, the \"Site\"). The assessment was authorized by Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager/CEO of Granite City Development LLC, pursuant to an engagement letter dated August 12, 2024.

The Phase II ESA was conducted to evaluate recognized environmental conditions (\"RECs\") identified in the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment previously prepared by GEC (GEC Project No. GEC-2024-0612, dated July 19, 2024) (the \"Phase I ESA\") and to characterize soil and groundwater quality across all three parcels comprising the Site. The Phase I ESA identified several RECs associated with historical industrial operations on the property, including petroleum storage, metal finishing operations involving chlorinated solvents, and the placement of fill material of uncertain provenance on portions of the Site. The present Phase II ESA was designed to investigate these RECs through a comprehensive subsurface sampling program, including the advancement of soil borings, installation of temporary groundwater monitoring wells, and laboratory analysis of soil and groundwater samples for a range of contaminants of concern.

The investigation is intended to support the Developer\'s due diligence in connection with the potential acquisition of the Site from the City of Lakemont, Illinois, for development of a mixed-use transit-oriented project adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station (the \"Lakemont Station Commons\" project). The Developer intends to acquire the consolidated Site from the City pursuant to a development agreement currently under negotiation.

The Phase II ESA was conducted in general conformance with ASTM Standard E1903-19, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and applicable Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") guidance documents, including the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\") Guidance Document (Publication 2024) and the IEPA Technical Guidance for Investigation and Remediation of Contaminated Sites. The subsurface investigation was designed to collect data sufficient to evaluate site conditions against the remediation objectives established under the IEPA Tiered Approach to Corrective Action Objectives (\"TACO\"), codified at 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 742.

The TACO framework establishes three tiers of remediation objectives for soil and groundwater. Tier 1 objectives are generic, risk-based standards applicable statewide. Tier 2 objectives are site-specific standards developed using modified exposure parameters or institutional and engineering controls. Tier 3 objectives are derived from a site-specific risk assessment. The analytical results from this investigation have been compared to Tier 1 remediation objectives for both residential and industrial/commercial land use scenarios, as well as to Class I groundwater quality standards established under 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 620.

The findings of this Phase II ESA will be used to determine whether enrollment in the IEPA VSRP is warranted for any or all of the three parcels and whether a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter can be obtained for the Site or individual parcels upon completion of any necessary remedial activities. The VSRP provides a voluntary, incentive-based mechanism for investigation and remediation of contaminated properties under IEPA oversight, culminating in the issuance of an NFR letter upon satisfactory completion of approved remedial actions.

**A. Establishing Ordinance and Statutory Authority**

The Lakemont Central TIF District was established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, adopted by the City Council on September 17, 2019, pursuant to the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.* The City of Lakemont is a home rule municipality with a population of approximately 87,000, exercising home rule powers under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970, in addition to the statutory TIF authority conferred by the TIF Act. The TIF district was designated on the basis of findings that the redevelopment project area qualified as a conservation area under the eligibility criteria then applicable under the TIF Act, including findings relating to obsolescence, deterioration, inadequate utilities, and environmental contamination. The Lakemont Central TIF District encompasses the project site and a number of surrounding parcels, including the area immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station.

**B. District Boundaries and Included Parcels**

The three subject parcels --- 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 --- are located within the Lakemont Central TIF District boundaries. The parcels are to be consolidated into a single legal parcel prior to or concurrent with the land conveyance to the Developer under the forthcoming development agreement.

**C. TIF District Duration and Expiration**

**The Lakemont Central TIF District expires on December 31, 2042.** Under the TIF Act, the maximum statutory life of a TIF district is twenty-three (23) years from the date of the ordinance designating the redevelopment project area. """Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

Developer shall have the cure periods set forth in Section 13.2 (90 days, plus additional time if diligently pursuing cure), followed by the Construction Lender's additional cure rights under Article 16 (not less than 60 days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with extension if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure, including time necessary to obtain possession through foreclosure or a receiver).

(c) **Contractual Repurchase Option.** If, after expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), Developer has failed to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion and the Construction Lender has not cured or completed Phase I, the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Property, exercisable only upon (i) Developer's failure to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion within the agreed timeframe, (ii) expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), and (iii) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or full satisfaction of the outstanding Construction Loan balance. The repurchase price shall be the lesser of (A) the original purchase price paid by Developer ($9,200,000) or (B) the then-current fair market value of the Property as determined by an independent MAI appraiser mutually selected by the parties. The repurchase option shall be subordinate to the Construction Lender's mortgage and shall not be exercisable until the Construction Lender's mortgage is satisfied or the Construction Lender consents.

(d) **Enhanced Performance Security.** The $15,000,000 Phase I performance security under Article 8 shall be available to the City as liquidated security for Developer's Phase I completion obligation, in addition to the repurchase option. The performance security shall be the City's primary financial remedy for a Phase I completion failure, to be drawn upon only after expiration of all cure periods.

(e) **No Automatic Reversion.** Title to the Property shall not automatically revert to the City under any circumstance. The City's remedies for a Phase I completion failure are limited to the contractual remedies set forth in this Article 14 (the repurchase option and the performance security) and the termination and damage remedies under Article 13, in each case subject to the Construction Lender's rights under Article 16.

[Drafting Note — Conflict C1 (reversionary interest — central conflict). This Article 14 is the most critical provision in this draft and the single most important open item. Resolution Condition #8(h) requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest with automatic reversion of fee simple title if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet Sections 4.2, 7.1(1), 12(a), and the "Specific Concern" discussion in Section 12) states unequivocally that it "cannot commit to a loan secured by a mortgage on property that is subject to a reversionary interest or any form of defeasible fee estate" and that incorporation of the reversionary interest "as stated in the Resolution, would make the Loan impossible to close." The Construction Lender suggests five alternative mechanisms: (i) a contractual repurchase option exercisable only after cure periods and Construction Lender consent/satisfaction; (ii) a performance escrow/additional letter of credit; (iii) liquidated damages; (iv) an enhanced completion guaranty; and (v) a right of first refusal subordinate to the mortgage. Section 14.2 adopts a combination of alternatives (i) and (ii) (repurchase option plus the existing $15,000,000 performance security), which the Construction Lender specifically identified as acceptable. This must be resolved before execution; if the City insists on the literal reversionary interest, the Construction Loan cannot close and the Project financing fails.]

### Section 21.2 Confidentiality; FOIA.

The terms and contents of this Agreement are confidential and shall not be disclosed by either party to any third party, except: (a) to each party's legal counsel, financial advisors, accountants, lenders, and equity investors, as reasonably necessary; (b) as required by applicable law or governmental regulation, including the Illinois Freedom of Information Act, 5 ILCS 140/, as applicable to the City; and (c) to the extent necessary for City Council proceedings, committee meetings, and other governmental approvals. The parties acknowledge that, as a public contract, this Agreement may be subject to disclosure under the Illinois Freedom of Information Act.

### Section 21.3 Amendments.

This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by a written instrument executed by both parties, subject to the Construction Lender's consent rights under Section 16.2 and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP's consent rights under the Lakeshore Side Letter.

### Section 21.4 Severability.

If any provision of this Agreement is held invalid or unenforceable, such invalidity shall not affect other provisions that can be given effect without the invalid provision, and the provisions are severable.

### Section 21.5 Entire Agreement; Supersession.

This Agreement, together with its exhibits and the documents referenced herein, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes the Term Sheet in its entirety upon execution. In the event of any conflict between this Agreement and the Term Sheet, this Agreement controls.

The completeness audit is complete. Here is a summary of what the audit found and fixed.

## Completeness Audit — Results

I re-read all seven source documents and cross-checked every distinct issue against the actual output file content (via grep on the extracted text, not memory). The first draft was solid but had **11 additional distinct points the record supports** that were omitted, bundled, or only implied. I added each with its own explicit treatment and source citation:

| # | Gap found | Source | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| C41 | Collateral assignment of DA + TIF rights to Lender | Loan TS §4.1 | New §16.7 operative provision + Exhibit O |
| C42 | TIF withholding as prevailing-wage remedy | TIF Memo §V.C | New §9.3(d) |
| O16 | Pre-remediation plume delineation cost | ESA §8.3 | New §6.3(f) |
| C43 | Lakeshore $268.4M indebtedness cap + no-lien | SL §§7(a),7(b) | New note in §7.4 |
| C44 | Lakeshore 2035 overall-completion trigger | SL §§4.3(c),7(c) | New note in §12.1 |
| C45 | Resolution "net of damages" refund concept | Res. Cond. #8(h) | New note in §14.2(c) |
| C46 | Loan default on recording lien/reversion | Loan TS §9(11) | New note in Art. 14 |
| C47 | Lakeshore CoC consequences (repurchase/mgmt right) | SL §5.4 | New note in §15.2 |
| O17 | Environmental covenants & access agreements | Res. §6 | New §6.4(d) + Exhibit N |
| — | Take-out/guaranty/interest-reserve open items | Loan TS §§1,2.2–2.4 | Added to §7.2 body |
| — | Clearmont equity certification | Res. Cond. #8(a) | Strengthened §2.2(d) |

I also verified all **8 Resolution conditions (a–h)**, all **6 TIF Memo recommendations**, all **4 ESA transactional recommendations**, and the **Lakeshore specific consent triggers** are explicitly addressed.

The consolidated index now lists **47 cross-document conflicts (C1–C47)** and **17 open items (O1–O17)**; drafting notes rose from 46 to 56. The regenerated `development-agreement-draft.docx` re-passed `validate.py` (exit 0) with all 22 articles and tables intact.
